entities.name: śakti
entities.cite: The power residing in words themselves is what binds this universe

entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: The power residing in words themselves

entities.name: ātman
entities.cite: this self, whose essence is the flash of insight

entities.name: bheda
entities.cite: is perceived in the form of difference

axioms.name: asrita
axioms.cite: the sakti lodged sabdesv eva, in words themselves

axioms.name: svarupa
axioms.cite: the atman, pratibha-atma, whose essence is pratibha — the immediate flash of understanding

axioms.name: netra
axioms.cite: sees *through* that word-power (yan-netra, 'whose eye is it')

axioms.name: bheda-rupa
axioms.cite: pratiyate bheda-rupah, is perceived in the form of difference

denials.name: bheda
denials.cite: an undivided seer, seeing through the instrument of the word-power, appears divided *because its eye is the differentiating instrument*

claims.name: bheda-rupa
claims.cite: is perceived in the form of difference

rejected_readings.label: atman_truly_divided
rejected_readings.rendering: The self is truly divided into many forms; difference is its real nature.
rejected_readings.why: The commentary explicitly states the seer is undivided (akhaṇḍa) and only *appears* divided because its differentiating instrument (the word-power) colours its perception. Making bheda an actual property of ātman contradicts the vivarta structure where multiplicity arises from the lens, not from the subject.

rejected_readings.label: sakti_without_instrument_role
rejected_readings.rendering: The word-power stands apart from the self and independently generates the appearance of the universe.
rejected_readings.why: Severs the constitutive unity in 'whose eye is that power': the commentary emphasizes that the power IS the self's very organ of self-perception, not an external force. The atman perceives *through* the word-power as its instrument (netra); they are inseparable in the perceptual act.

rejected_readings.label: parinama_appearance
rejected_readings.rendering: The atman actually transforms (parinama) into the differentiated world through the word-power's action.
rejected_readings.why: The verse's metaphor of the eye colouring the seen denies real transformation. The atman remains undivided; the appearance of difference is an artefact of the perceptual instrument (word-power), not a real change in the ātman's substance.

notes: Verse 1.118 answers 1.1 by explaining non-duality's apparent paradox: how the one Brahman-ātman appears as the many. The mechanism is not substance-transformation (parinama) but perceptual appearance through the word-power's differentiating lens (vivarta). The verse deliberately preserves the parinama/vivarta ambiguity opened in 1.1 by using 'bheda-rupa pratiyate' — whether difference is illusory appearance or real manifestation remains contested and foundational to Advaita's later development.
